mnemonics.title: UNIX Permission Order
mnemonics.content: READ = 4, WRITE = 2, EXECUTE = 1. REMEMBER: R(4) W(2) E(1) - THINK 'ROGER WE' OR 'READ WRITE EARN' - FOUR, TWO, ONE!

mnemonics.title: ACL vs Capabilities
mnemonics.content: ACL = A FILE-CENTRIC LIST (Column-wise). Capability = CARRY YOUR TICKET (Process-centric). A = Associate with Object, C = Carry with Process.

mnemonics.title: Permission Groups
mnemonics.content: U-G-O = USER (OWNER), GROUP, OTHERS. ALWAYS IN THIS ORDER!

mnemonics.title: Least Privilege
mnemonics.content: LEAST = Limited Access Every Specific Task. GIVE ONLY WHAT IS NEEDED, NOTHING MORE!

acronyms.usage: A list attached to each file specifying which users/groups can access it and their permissions.
Access control where owners can discretionarily grant/revoke access to their files (UNIX, Windows).
Access control based on classification levels enforced by the system (military, government systems).
Access control based on roles assigned to users (admin, manager, employee).
A unique number assigned to each user in UNIX systems for identification and permission checking.
A number identifying a group of users who share common file access permissions.
A special permission bit that allows program execution with file owner's privileges.
A special permission bit that allows program execution with file group's privileges.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a table with DOMAINS as ROWS and OBJECTS as COLUMNS. Each cell contains rights like READ, WRITE, EXECUTE. Think of it as a PERMISSION MAP!
Imagine THREE CIRCLES: Owner (inner), Group (middle), Others (outer). Each circle divided into three wedges: R, W, X. The more wedges you fill, the more power you have!
ACL = A clipboard attached to EACH FILE (column). Capability = An ID card carried by EACH USER (row). Easy to revoke = CLIPBOARD. Hard to revoke = ID CARD (copies everywhere!).

analogies.analogy: Like a GUEST LIST at an exclusive club. Each person (file) has their own list of who can enter and what they can do inside. Easy to update the list, hard to track everyone who got in.
Like a KEYCARD or VIP PASS. Each user carries their own card that opens certain doors. Easy to check at door, HARD TO REVOKE if copies exist.
Like a SECURITY CLEARANCE LEVEL. Someone with 'Top Secret' clearance can access more areas than someone with 'Confidential'. Switching domains = passing through different security checkpoints.
Like a HOTEL KEY CARD. Housekeeping gets a card that opens ONLY guest rooms, not the manager's office or the safe. They get MINIMUM access needed for their job.
